publisher.publisher-name: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tripoli

title-group.article-title: Abdominal radiographic features of anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity in 14 dogs and 2 cats

contrib-group.aff: 1Center Grove Animal Clinic, 2435 S State Rd 135, Greenwood, Indiana, 46143, USA
2Department of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine, Purdue University, 625 Harrison St., West Lafayette, Indiana, 47906, USA

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding Author: Masahiro Murakami. Department of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, Purdue University, 625 Harrison St, West Lafayette, IN 47907, USA. mmuraka@purdue.edu

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity is commonly encountered in veterinary practice that can result in internal bleeding. We have observed dogs with retroperitoneal hemorrhage secondary to anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity. However, abdominal radiographic changes in dogs with rodenticide toxicity have not been studied and retroperitoneal hemorrhage secondary to rodenticide toxicity has rarely been reported.

sec.title: Aim:
sec.p: The objective is to describe abdominal radiographic features of anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity and concurrent thoracic radiographic changes in dogs and cats.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: Dogs and cats diagnosed with rodenticide toxicity and with available abdominal radiographs were included in this retrospective analysis. Board-certified radiologists reviewed the abdominal and thoracic radiographs. Evaluation of abdominal radiographic changes included assessment of peritoneal or retroperitoneal effusion, subcutaneous hemorrhage, and internal hemorrhage of abdominal organs.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: Fourteen dogs and two cats with confirmed rodenticide toxicity were included in the study. In dogs, retroperitoneal effusion (28.6%) was the most commonly observed abdominal radiographic change, followed by peritoneal effusion (14.3%). Thoracic radiographic changes in dogs included pleural effusion (63.6%) and mediastinal widening (63.6%) as the most common findings, followed by pulmonary hemorrhage (36.4%) and tracheal narrowing (36.4%). Subcutaneous hemorrhage or edema (9.1%) was also noted. No abdominal radiographic changes consistent with hemorrhage secondary to rodenticide toxicity were noted in the two cats.

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: Based on our findings, it is suggested that rodenticide toxicity may result in retroperitoneal effusion even in the absence of thoracic disease. Therefore, abdominal radiographs may be valuable when suspecting hemorrhage due to coagulopathy. However, abdominal radiographic changes associated with rodenticide toxicity are considered rare in cats.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity is a commonly encountered condition in veterinary practice (Murphy, 2002; DeClementi and Sobczak, 2018). Anticoagulant rodenticides function by inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase recycling, which is necessary for the synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. These factors are involved in all pathways of the coagulation cascade, but initially affect the extrinsic pathway by limiting factor VII (Murphy, 2002). Ineffective intrinsic, extrinsic, and coagulation cascades result in prolonged clotting times and coagulopathy, and may lead to bleeding anywhere in the body (DeClementi and Sobczak, 2018). Hemorrhagic effusion may be present in either or both the thoracic and abdominal cavities (Murphy, 2002). However, hemorrhagic effusion in the retroperitoneal space secondary to anticoagulant rodenticide poisoning has rarely been described (Sheafor and Couto, 1999; Griggs et al., 2016). Thoracic and abdominal radiographs are occasionally performed in dogs and cats with suspected rodenticide poisoning to identify sites of hemorrhage.

Radiographic abnormalities in the thoracic cavity and upper airway in dogs with anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity have been well described, including upper airway obstruction due to extraluminal hemorrhage (Blocker and Roberts, 1999; Lawson et al., 2017; Thomer and Santoro Beer, 2018), mediastinal hemorrhage (Berry et al., 1993; Sheafor and Couto, 1999), pleural effusion (Berry et al., 1993; Sheafor and Couto, 1999), and pulmonary hemorrhage (Berry et al., 1993; Sheafor and Couto, 1999). Reported abdominal radiographic changes associated with rodenticide toxicity in dogs are sparse, including one case report of hematometra (Padgett et al., 1998) and a few cases of possible retroperitoneal effusion (Sheafor and Couto, 1999; Griggs et al., 2016). Thoracic and abdominal radiographs in cats with suspected anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity have only been described in one article; two cats with suspected mediastinal hemorrhage and one cat with pleural effusion (Kohn et al., 2003).

Although we have occasionally encountered dogs with retroperitoneal hemorrhage secondary to anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity, comprehensive abdominal radiographic changes in dogs with this condition have not been extensively studied. The purpose of this study was to describe the abdominal radiographic features of anticoagulant rodenticide toxicity and concurrent thoracic radiographic changes in dogs and cats.

sec.title: Case selection
sec.p: The Purdue University Veterinary Teaching Hospital medical records database from January 1, 2013, through July 31, 2020, was electronically searched to identify dogs and cats with a clinical diagnosis of rodenticide toxicity and abdominal radiography performed at the presentation. Medical records were further reviewed, and other diagnostic procedures performed to confirm radiographic changes were recorded. Thoracic radiographs, when available, were also included in the study.

sec.title: Radiographic findings
sec.p: Radiographs of the abdomen and thorax (if available) were reviewed by consensus in the form of digital files by two ACVR board-certified radiologists (M.M. and C.F.) using an image viewing workstation (Asteris Keystone viewer, Asteris Inc., Stephentown, NY).

Abdominal radiographic changes that may indicate hemorrhage due to coagulopathy secondary to rodenticide toxicity were evaluated, including peritoneal or retroperitoneal effusion, subcutaneous hemorrhage, and internal hemorrhage of abdominal organs. Peritoneal effusion was suspected when abdominal radiographs showed decreased or loss of serosal margination of intraperitoneal organs. Retroperitoneal effusion was suspected if there was soft tissue opacity in the retroperitoneal space with or without border effacement of the renal silhouettes and mass effect. Subcutaneous hemorrhage or edema was suspected if there was a wispy soft tissue opacity in the subcutaneous layer of the body wall. Organomegaly was recorded to identify possible internal hemorrhage in these organs. Each abnormality was subjectively assessed as mild, moderate, or severe.

The thoracic radiographic changes, which may indicate hemorrhage due to coagulopathy secondary to rodenticide toxicity, were also evaluated when thoracic radiographs were available. Tracheal narrowing secondary to extraluminal hemorrhage was suspected if there was increased soft tissue opacity adjacent to the trachea causing focal narrowing of the trachea. Pleural effusion was suspected if there was soft tissue opacity in the pleural space with retraction of the lung lobes from the thoracic walls, widening of the pleural fissure lines, and/or border effacement of the cardiac silhouette and diaphragm. Mediastinal effusion was suspected if there was a soft-tissue opaque widening of the cranial mediastinum. Pulmonary hemorrhage was suspected if there was ill-defined increased soft tissue opacity in the pulmonary parenchyma with or without an air bronchogram.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: The prevalence of each radiographic change was calculated using the following formula: Prevalence (%) = (number of dogs or cats with each radiographic change [A] / total number of dogs or cats with radiographs [T]) × 100. Binomial 95% confidence intervals of the prevalence were calculated using the following formula: 95% confidence intervals = [A/T ± 1.96√A/(T2)].

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Fourteen dogs and two cats with a confirmed diagnosis of rodenticide toxicity had abdominal radiographs at presentation. Of the 14 dogs and two cats with abdominal radiographs, 11 dogs and two cats also had thoracic radiographs at the same presentation. A summary of the data from this study is shown in Table 1.

Abdominal radiographs showed diffuse, wispy soft tissue opacity throughout the peritoneal space with decreased serosal margination in two dogs. A hemorrhagic peritoneal effusion was confirmed by ultrasound-guided abdominocentesis (PCV of 48%) in one dog (Fig. 1A and 1B). Thoracic radiography was not performed in this dog at the same presentation. The other dog did not have further confirmation of peritoneal effusion by abdominal ultrasound or ultrasound-guided aspiration. Thoracic radiographs were performed on this dog, and tracheal narrowing, pleural effusion, and mediastinal hemorrhage were observed on thoracic radiographs (Fig. 2A and 2B), and hemorrhagic pleural effusion was confirmed by fine needle aspiration.

Radiographic findings of retroperitoneal effusion were present in four dogs. Abdominal ultrasound was performed in two dogs and retroperitoneal effusion was confirmed. None of the dogs with retroperitoneal effusion had radiographic evidence of peritoneal effusion. In one dog with retroperitoneal effusion, a large amount of soft tissue opacity was present in the retroperitoneal space with complete effacement of the bilateral renal silhouettes and ventral displacement of the intestines (Fig. 3A and 3B), and retroperitoneal effusion was confirmed by abdominal ultrasound. Thoracic radiography was not performed in this dog, and abdominal radiographs were repeated 6 days later with a marked reduction of the retroperitoneal effusion (Fig. 3C and 3D). In three additional dogs with retroperitoneal effusion, only a small amount of soft tissue opacity was present in the retroperitoneal space with partial effacement of the renal silhouettes without mass effect. Thoracic radiographs were obtained in all three dogs, with one of the three dogs showing evidence of pleural and mediastinal effusion. The remaining two dogs had normal thoracic radiographs.

caption.title: This table presents the compiled data from a retrospective study of dogs and cats diagnosed with rodenticide toxicity who underwent abdominal radiography. Medical records were reviewed from January 1, 2013 through July 31, 2020. The study includes 14 dogs, 11 of which underwent thoracic radiography, and 2 cats, both of which underwent thoracic radiography.

Ventrodorsal (A) and left lateral (B) views of abdominal radiographs in a dog with rodenticide toxicity. Diffuse wispy increased soft tissue opacity throughout the peritoneal cavity with decreased serosal margination was present. Hemorrhagic peritoneal effusion was confirmed using ultrasound guided aspiration.

Ventrodorsal (A) and left lateral (B) views of thoracic radiographs in a dog with rodenticide toxicity. Dorsoventral narrowing of cervical trachea, soft tissue opacity in the pleural cavity with lungs retracted from the thoracic wall and widening of cranial mediastinum with soft tissue opacity were present. Hemorrhagic pleural effusion was confirmed via fine needle aspiration.

sec.p: Eight dogs and two cats had no radiographic abnormalities on abdominal radiographs. Thoracic radiographs were available for seven of the eight dogs and one of the two cats with normal abdominal radiographs. Among these animals, tracheal narrowing was present in three dogs, pleural effusion in five dogs, mediastinal widening in five dogs, and pulmonary hemorrhage in four dogs. The one cat with normal abdominal radiographs also had normal thoracic radiographs.

In dogs, the radiographic finding of retroperitoneal effusion (28.6%, 4/14) was the most common abdominal radiographic change, followed by peritoneal effusion (14.3%, 2/14). There were no dogs with radiographic findings of subcutaneous hemorrhage or edema on the abdominal radiographs. Pleural effusion (63.6%, 7/11) and mediastinal widening (63.6%, 7/11) were the most common thoracic radiographic findings in dogs, followed by pulmonary hemorrhage (36.4%, 4/11) and tracheal narrowing (36.4%, 4/11), and subcutaneous hemorrhage or edema (9.1%, 1/11). In two cats, there were no abdominal radiographic changes consistent with hemorrhage. One cat had a pleural effusion. Tracheal narrowing, mediastinal widening, and pulmonary hemorrhage were not present in any cat. The prevalence and binomial 95% confidence intervals for each radiographic finding on abdominal and thoracic radiography in dogs with rodenticide toxicity are shown in Table 2.

caption.title: Ventrodorsal (A, C) and left lateral (B, D) views of abdominal radiographs in a dog with rodenticide toxicity at initial presentation (A, B) and 6 days later (C, D). A, B; A large amount of soft tissue opacity (asterisk) was present in the retroperitoneal space with complete effacement of the renal silhouette and ventral displacement of the intestines. Retroperitoneal effusion was confirmed via abdominal ultrasound. C, D; The previous soft tissue opacity in the retroperitoneal space is markedly reduced with partial effacement of the renal silhouette and resolution of mass effect.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study highlights clinically significant radiographic findings in dogs diagnosed with rodenticide toxicity and underscores the diagnostic value of abdominal radiography. We found that the most common radiographic manifestations in these dogs were retroperitoneal and peritoneal effusions, which occurred in 28.6% and 14.2% of cases, respectively. In contrast, these effusions were absent in all cats included in this study. While previous research has focused primarily on thoracic radiographic findings in canine rodenticide toxicity (Berry et al., 1993), our study is the first to document the prevalence of retroperitoneal effusions in this population.

Reduced retroperitoneal detail, which may indicate retroperitoneal hemorrhage, has only been documented in a limited number of dogs with rodenticide toxicity (Griggs et al., 2016; Sheafor and Couto, 1999). For example, one of seven dogs showed decreased retroperitoneal detail, but without additional confirmation of retroperitoneal effusion (Sheafor and Couto, 1999). In our study, retroperitoneal effusion was confirmed by ultrasound in two dogs, likely associated with rodenticide toxicity.

In the previous study, the dog with suspected retroperitoneal effusion showed minimal thoracic changes; one dog showed patchy alveolar infiltration in the lungs and minimal pleural effusion on thoracic radiographs (Sheafor and Couto, 1999), and in another study (Griggs et al., 2016), no thoracic radiographic abnormalities were found in the dogs in that study. The dogs in the present study with reduced retroperitoneal detail on abdominal radiographs had minimal corresponding thoracic changes. For example, only one had pleural effusion and mediastinal widening, and another had subcutaneous edema or hemorrhage in the thoracic body wall. These observations suggest that rodenticide toxicity may result in retroperitoneal effusion independent of thoracic disease.

caption.title: The prevalence and binomial 95% confidence intervals for each radiographic finding on abdominal and thoracic radiography in dogs with rodenticide toxicity.

sec.p: Causes of retroperitoneal effusion in dogs and cats include conditions such as acute renal injury, renal neoplasia, nephritis, renal abscess, urinary tract obstruction, or adrenal or other retroperitoneal tumors such as hemangiosarcoma (Stoneham et al., 2004). However, in the dogs we studied with reduced retroperitoneal detail, no signs of renal disease were observed, and abdominal ultrasound did not reveal any adrenal abnormalities. This suggests that the observed retroperitoneal hemorrhages were likely due to coagulopathy resulting from rodenticide toxicity.

While previous studies have recognized peritoneal effusion as a frequent site of internal hemorrhage from rodenticide toxicity (Sheafor and Couto, 1999; Murphy, 2018), our findings show a lower prevalence of peritoneal effusion (14.2%) than previously reported (57.1%; Sheafor and Couto, 1999). This difference remains unclear.

Therefore, abdominal radiographs may be valuable in cases where hemorrhage due to coagulopathy is suspected, even when thoracic radiographs appear normal. This could provide critical radiographic evidence of internal bleeding and support the diagnosis of rodenticide toxicity.

Moreover, in previous research evaluating thoracic radiographic abnormalities linked to rodenticide toxicity, pleural effusion was the most frequent thoracic radiographic change, followed by pulmonary infiltrates and mediastinal soft tissue opacities (Berry et al., 1993). Our findings align with these observations but also extend the understanding of potential thoracic radiographic changes resulting from rodenticide toxicity.

Previous studies have also documented subcutaneous hematomas in 13% of dogs diagnosed with rodenticide toxicity (Sheafor and Couto, 1999). The current study similarly evaluated subcutaneous changes seen on thoracic or abdominal radiographs.

In terms of rodenticide toxicity in cats, it is notably rarer than in dogs, with minimal documented cases (Hornfeldt and Murphy, 1997; Kohn et al., 2003). Consistent with past studies, our retrospective review identified very few cats with rodenticide toxicity, and abdominal radiographs in these cases showed no abnormalities. This rarity and lack of detectable abdominal changes warrant further research with a larger sample size.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In conclusion, our research highlights the significant association between abdominal radiographic changes, particularly retroperitoneal and peritoneal effusions, and rodenticide toxicity in dogs. Notably, the presence of such abdominal changes is often observed in dogs with radiographically unremarkable thorax, leading us to propose the inclusion of abdominal radiographs as a component of the diagnostic process for canine rodenticide toxicity to improve diagnostic accuracy and outcomes. Despite the limitations of a small sample size, abdominal radiographic changes are uncommon in cats with rodenticide toxicity.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest with respect to the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors received no financial support for the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

sec.title: Authors’ contributions
sec.p: Conceptualization, K.H., C.F. and M.M.; methodology, K.H., C.F. and M.M.; data acquisition, K.H., C.F. and M.M.; validation, C.F. and M.M.; formal analysis, K.H., C.F. and M.M.; investigation, K.H., C.F. and M.M.; resources, C.F. and M.M.; writing—original draft preparation, K.H.; writing—review and editing, C.F. and M.M.; visualization, C.F. and M.M.; supervision, M.M.; project administration, M.M. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
